candidates.expected_qualitative_behavior: Likely the strongest of these three proposals if the workload benefits from exposing more N-side parallelism while avoiding the extreme row underfill risk of very tall arrays.
candidates.mechanism_hypothesis: For these compact GEMMs, a moderately wide array should better match the workload because the declared layers are consistently broader in N than in M, so more columns can be kept busy than rows under the same PE budget.
candidates.anticipated_failure_mode: If the mapping or wavefront effects make 64 columns too wide for these small tiles, or if utilization is dominated by K-tiling and drain/fill overhead rather than M-versus-N fit, its wider shape may not convert into the best decision score.
candidates.rationale: This shape keeps the full 1024-PE budget while leaning wide instead of square. It tests the hypothesis that the local score is helped more by matching larger N than by preserving symmetry.

candidates.expected_qualitative_behavior: Potentially best only if N-dominant parallelism strongly outweighs all startup, tail, and tiling penalties; otherwise it is a higher-variance version of the wide-shape idea.
candidates.mechanism_hypothesis: If column-side occupancy is the main bottleneck, pushing the geometry to the widest legal extreme could reduce effective N-side underutilization further than 16x64.
candidates.anticipated_failure_mode: This shape may be too extreme for the smaller layers, causing excessive imbalance, more frequent partial-column use, or worse fill/drain behavior that offsets its width advantage.
candidates.rationale: Including the widest legal nonbaseline shape gives the study a clean test of whether the workload truly rewards aggressive width or prefers a less extreme wide array.

candidates.expected_qualitative_behavior: Likely worse than the wider proposals if N-side matching dominates, but it could be competitive if moderate row count reduces wavefront waste and improves effective utilization on the smallest layers.
candidates.mechanism_hypothesis: A somewhat taller, smaller-footprint rectangle may recover utilization on compact tiles if very wide arrays suffer disproportionate startup and tail inefficiency.
candidates.anticipated_failure_mode: Because aggregate and per-layer N exceed M, this shape may leave potential column parallelism unused and lose on both cycles and utilization-adjusted score.
candidates.rationale: This candidate is an intentional counterpoint to the wide options. It checks whether the apparent N-heavy aspect ratio is genuinely decisive once compact-layer mapping effects are included.

global_mechanism_hypothesis: Under weight-stationary GEMM for this compact workload, array shape is likely decided more by how well the geometry matches the M-versus-N footprint of each layer than by PE count alone. Because the declared layers are broader in N than in M, and two layers have especially small M values, wider arrays should generally achieve better effective occupancy than taller arrays at the same PE budget, unless extreme width introduces enough fill, drain, or tiling inefficiency to erase that advantage.

discriminating_test.original_workload_prediction: On the declared workload, 16x64 should outperform 64x16 on the preregistered decision score because the workload is N-leaning and compact in M, so the wider geometry should waste less parallel capacity than the taller one.
discriminating_test.transposed_workload_prediction: After transposing each layer so M and N are swapped, 64x16 should outperform 16x64 on the same score because the aspect-ratio advantage should reverse when the workload becomes M-leaning instead of N-leaning.
discriminating_test.falsifier: This mechanism is falsified if the winner does not reverse under transposition, or if 64x16 matches or beats 16x64 on the original workload while 16x64 also matches or beats 64x16 on the transposed workload.

evidence_boundary: This proposal is a bounded preregistered hypothesis, not a simulator result. It does not claim general superiority of AI proposals, general XR performance, implementation readiness, physical feasibility, power or thermal properties, or product fitness. Any accepted conclusion must come only from the runner's gated SCALE-Sim evaluations and the separate shared mechanism probe.
